·-' tate of Ca iforIlia 
-
air Political Practices CommissioIl 
Technical Assistance/Administration 
(916) 322.5660 
••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Executive/legal 
322-5901 
Enforcement/Conflict of Interest 
322-6441 
August 2, 1976 
John F. Van DeKamp 
District Attorney 
County of Los Angeles 
Office of the District Attorney 
18000 Criminal Courts Building 
210 West Temple Street 
Los Angeles 
CA 
90012 
Dear Mr. Van DeKamp: 
Thank you for your letter of July 20, 1976, inquiring 
about the applicability of the opinion requested by the 
Honorable Kenneth Cory, 1 FPPC Ops. 137 (No. 75-094-C, Oct. 1, 
1975) to the situation where an elected officer attends, at no 
expense, fundraising dinners or testimonials conducted for 
charitable purposes. 
It is the view of the staff that free 
admission and incidental benefits which are provided in 
connection with an elected officer's attendance at the types 
of functions to which you refer will not create any reporting 
obligations under the Political Reform Act. 
Attendance at such functions by an elected officer involves 
what is essentially a political activity and, therefore, would be 
reportable, if at all, under Chapter 4 of the Political Reform 
Act. 
In light of the fact that Chapter 4 contains the relevant 
reporting criteria, the reasoning employed in the opinion requested 
by Mr. Cory is applicable to the types of functions you describe 
and the attendance of the elected officer constitutes adequate 
consideration for the benefits he receives. 
If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate 
to contact me. 
1 Division 


COUNTY OF LOS ANGELES 
OFFICE OF THE DISTRICT ATTORNEY 
18000 CRIMINAL COURTS BUILDING 
210 WEST TEMPLE STREET 
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90012 
974-3501 
JOH N K. VAN DE KAM P, DISTRICT ATTORNEY 
July 20, 1976 
Mr. Kenneth Finney, Director 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Dear Mr. Finney: 
I have received and read with interest the Commission's 
Opinion No. 75-094-C wherein the Commission responds to an 
inquiry by The Honorable Kenneth Cory_ 
The conclusion of 
tiLe Commission was as follows: 
IIIf an elected state officer attends a political 
fund raising dinner at the invitation of the sponsors 
without purchasing a ticket, the officer has no report-
ing obligations under the Political Reform Act. 
This 
conclusion is not altered by the fact that the officer 
attends the dinner for the specified purpose of making 
a speech." 
An elected officer also receives, with some frequency, 
invitations from sponsors to attend, at no expense, fund 
raising dinners or testimonials conducted for charitable 
purposes. 
I would assume that the same reasoning applies to this 
situation as to the previous question posed by Mr. Cory. 
Again, by virtue of his attendance, a benefit constituting 
adequate consideration is conferred upon the sponsors by 
the invi 
officer. 
Hence, no reporting obligation under 
the Political Reform Act arises. 
I write to inquire if 
this assumption is correct. 
Sincerely, 
row 
cc: 
Richard Miller, Esq. 
Compliance and Enforcement Section 
Fair Political Practices Commission

sections.question_synthetic: Does an elected officer's free attendance at fundraising dinners or testimonials conducted for charitable purposes create any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Free admission and incidental benefits from an elected officer's attendance at charitable fundraising dinners create no reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act. The officer's attendance constitutes adequate consideration for any benefits received, and the reasoning from the Cory opinion applies to such charitable functions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Complete advice letter from FPPC in response to inquiry from District Attorney. Both question and conclusion sections are clearly present. Document quality is good despite OCR notation. References prior opinion No. 75-094-C by Kenneth Cory.

I would assume that the same reasoning applies to this situation as to the previous question posed by Mr. Cory. Again, by virtue of his attendance, a benefit constituting adequate consideration is conferred upon the sponsors by the invited officer. Hence, no reporting obligation under the Political Reform Act arises. I write to inquire if this assumption is correct.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the Los Angeles District Attorney that an elected officer's free attendance at charitable fundraising dinners or testimonials does not create reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act, as the officer's attendance provides adequate consideration for any benefits received.
